query: is abc g2 a transmembrane protein

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Biliary -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: There are specific transporters on the canalicular membrane of the hepatocytes for the secretion of bile acids. For instance, the bile salt export pump carries the ATP-dependent bile acid efflux (BSEP). In addition, there is apical ATP binding cassette (ABC) transporters, including multidrug resistance-P glycoprotein 3 (MDR3) for phospholipids, MDR1 for lipophilic cationic drugs, multidrug resistance-associated protein 2 (MRP2) for non-bile acid organic anions, and the protein ABCG5/G8 for the secretion of cholesterol and other sterols. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Drug Transporters and Genes
retrieved_docs.content: ATP-binding cassette transporters: ABC transporters A-D and G are transmembrane proteins that transport both exogenous and endogenous compounds, including sterols, lipids, and drugs. These transporters contribute to essential physiological processes such as lipoprotein biogenesis, detoxification of toxins, or resistance to drugs. Some are even involved in disease pathways resulting from transporter dysfunction. Humans have 48 ABC transporter genes, classified into 7 subfamilies (ABCA to ABCG), which encode proteins that mediate the transport of diverse endogenous and exogenous substances, including lipids and drugs. [41] Among these transporters, ABCB1, also known as P-gp, is a key efflux pump that has been extensively studied for its role in drug transport and multidrug resistance.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bile -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The mediation of the uptake of bile acids from the sinusoids is via Na-dependent bile acid transporters and Na-independent bile acid transporters. The Na-dependent bile acid transporter is called sodium taurocholate-cotransporting polypeptide (NTCP). NCTP does not express at the canalicular membrane; it only expresses at the basolateral membrane. The Na-independent mechanism involves organic anion transporting polypeptides (OATPs) transporter. OATP transporter transports bile acids into the hepatocytes in exchange for bicarbonate or glutathione. Certain Na-independent bile acid transporters (OST/alpha and OST/beta) are present at the basal membrane of cholangiocytes and hepatocytes. In the cholangiocytes, these transporters may participate in the cholehepatic shunt pathway (explained above), while in the hepatocytes, these transporters may participate in bile acid efflux into the bloodstream in cases of bile accumulation in the hepatocytes (cholestasis). After the uptake of bile acids from the sinusoids or after they are synthesized endogenously from cholesterol, the unconjugated and conjugated bile acids get secreted into the canaliculi through a special energy-dependent transporter called bile salt export pump (BSEP). BSEP not only transports bile acids into the canaliculi, but it also aids in the excretion of certain drugs. The BAIF of bile flow is mainly determined by the ATP-dependent transport of glutathione through the multidrug resistance-associated protein 2 (MRP2). Cholesterol and sterols are secreted by ABCG5/G8 protein, while phospholipids and lipophilic cationic drugs are secreted by multidrug resistance-P glycoprotein 3 (MDR-3) and MDR1, respectively. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Progressive Familial Intrahepatic Cholestasis -- Pathophysiology -- PFIC3
retrieved_docs.content: PFIC3 occurs due to a defective ABCB4 gene located on chromosome 7. This gene encodes for a translocator protein involved in phosphatidylcholine excretion. It is thought that these patients have prolonged exposure to hydrophobic bile salts since there is a lack of phospholipids, resulting in hepatocellular damage. The majority of these defective genes result in a shortened protein. A study performed in 50 patients known to have PFIC3 demonstrated that about a third of the tested patients had no detected MDR3 P-glycoproteins within the liver, likely related to rapid destruction of the prematurely formed protein from an early stop codon within the genetic sequence. Newer studies are now discovering that these MDR3 defects, similar to PFIC1 and 2, reflect a spectrum upon which this variant falls upon and may even be related to other conditions such as adult idiopathic cirrhosis, drug-induced cholestasis, and transient neonatal cholestasis. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histocompatibility Antigen -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Major histocompatibility complex molecules are initially synthesized in and anchored to the rough endoplasmic reticulum (ER). After MHC class I chains are synthesized, they are first bound to calnexin, a membrane-bound molecule in the rough ER that stabilizes the chain until it can bind to beta2-microglobulin. After synthesis, chaperone proteins calreticulin, tapasin, and ERp57 additionally stabilize the molecule until an antigenic peptide binds. Proteins synthesized in the cytoplasm are then cleaved into peptides about 8 to 10 amino acids long and transported to the ER by ATP-dependent transporter proteins TAP1 or TAP2, where they can bind to MHC class I molecules. Any antigen that produces intracellular proteins, whether viral, bacterial, or tumor in etiology, could bind to MHC. Also, defective proteins that did not fold correctly and are digested by proteasomes, also known as defective ribosomal products (DRiPs), could bind to MHC. Tapasin is a molecule involved in stabilizing the transfer of peptides from TAP transporters to MHC molecules. Once the MHC I-peptide complex forms, it is transported to the cell surface, where it could potentially bind to a cytotoxic T cell and induce a response. In most healthy cells, if the complex contains a self-antigen, no immune response will be triggered. However, if the CD8 T cell identifies a non-self antigen, then it will trigger cell lysis. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bile Secretion -- Cellular Level -- Canalicular Membrane
retrieved_docs.content: The transporter proteins found in the canalicular membrane are primarily members of the ATP-binding cassette protein family. [9] These proteins use active transport to secrete molecules and enzymes into the bile. These transporter proteins include the bile salt export pump (BSEP), the multispecific organic anion transporter (MRP2), the multiple drug resistance 1 and 3 (MDR1 and MDR3), ATP dependent phospholipid transporter (flippase), ATP-dependent transporter of organic cations, and the canalicular bicarbonate transporter. [9] The canalicular membrane transporters help secrete molecules into bile against concentration gradients and enzymes such as alkaline phosphatase. Contractile microfilaments facilitate the secretion of bile through the canaliculi. The canalicular membrane accounts for only 1% of the surface area of the hepatocyte.

retrieved_docs.title: BAX Gene -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Bax protein exists constitutively in the cytoplasm until activated. It binds to multiple anti-apoptotic proteins, such as Bcl-xl, that inhibit Bax translocation to the MOM; therefore, Bax-mediated apoptosis depends on the concentration of both pro- and anti-apoptotic proteins. [6] Bax becomes activated by binding upregulated death signals such as BH3-only proteins or t-Bid at its BH3 hydrophobic pocket domain. Activation causes conformational changes that lead to translocation to the MOM, where Bax induces mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP) by 2 possible mechanisms described above. [6] [2] MOMP leads to mitochondrial swelling and rupture, causing the release of periplasmic proteins such as cytochrome C. [2] Cytochrome C binds and activates cytosolic caspases, which are known to be the effector proteases of cell death. [2] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Tangier Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations have been found in chromosome 9q31, which encodes for the ATP-Binding cassette transporter (ABCA-1) gene. These transporters normally transmit cholesterol and phospholipids from inside the cells into the liver. Thus cholesterol will be accumulated within the cells, which eventually impairs cell functions and lead to their death. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastric Intrinsic Factor -- Mechanism -- Ileal Events
retrieved_docs.content: After its recognition by cubilin, the IF-Cbl complex gets endocytosed into the enterocyte bound to the cubam complex. Inside the endosome, the IF-Cbl complex gets liberated, and cubam gets recycled to the apical membrane. Within the lysosome, Cbl is released from IF by the action of lysosomal cathepsin L. [23] Cbl will be exported from the lysosome to the cytoplasm via a transporter called LMBD1. [24] The transport of Cbl to the bloodstream is mediated by ABC transporter multidrug resistance protein 1 (MRP1) located in the basolateral membrane of ileal cells. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Drug Transporters and Genes
retrieved_docs.content: Drug transporters are involved in drug absorption, distribution, and elimination, as they determine a drug's fate in the body. These transporters are proteins that regulate the transportation of drugs by facilitating or restricting their movement across cellular membranes. The genes and variants of these transporters, subject to the nature of polymorphism, may exhibit different transport behaviors and result in varied outcomes. Drug-transporting systems are classified into 2 major families as follows: ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters are active efflux mechanisms that utilize ATP to extrude drugs against concentration gradients. Solute carrier (SLC) transporters facilitate passive or secondary active transport of drugs across cellular membranes. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Chloride Channels -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanism of activation has been the primary mode of categorization of chloride channels. However, this is problematic because of overlap and lack of information on structural similarities of the corresponding proteins. Molecular cloning has generated a considerable amount of data that will eventually help in classifying them structurally. However, this is far from being complete. Thus far, three major structural classes have been identified. Firstly, GABA and glycine receptors (ligand-gated Cl- channels) assemble as pentamers, and each monomer containing four transmembrane spans. CFTR is the only chloride channel member of the second class and has about 12 transmembrane spans, arranged in two domains, two nucleotide-binding folds (NBFs), and a regulatory R domain. Although CFTR belongs to the gene family of ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters, which generally function as active transporters driven by ATP hydrolysis. This Cl- channel works as a cyclic AMP-activated CI- channel. Last class includes the gene family of CLC Cl- channels, which have about 12 transmembrane domains, each monomer has a pore (double-barreled channels) and function as voltage-gated channels. There is proof that these three structural classes are Cl- channels beyond a reasonable doubt. However, there are suggestions that four other unrelated proteins may also be CI- channels (p64, Ca-CC, phospholemman, pICln). [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Progressive Familial Intrahepatic Cholestasis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: As a result of mutations within the hepatocellular system, individuals have impaired bile formation resulting in impairment of the mechanisms of secretion, which results in signs and symptoms of cholestasis. Three known subtypes exist, the first two PFIC1 and PFIC2, often present within the first few months after birth, while PFIC3 often presents early in childhood. Three known genes have been discovered that are responsible for this condition. Just as PFIC1 and PFIC 2 present early in life, they also share a defective gene, ATP8B1, and ABCB11. On the contrary, these aforementioned genes are thought to be intact in PFIC3, rather this variant has defects in ABCB4, a gene involved in the production of a protein known as multi-drug resistant 3 which is part of a group of proteins known as “flippases” which assist in translocation of a variety of phospholipids from cell membranes into the bile. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: DJS comes from mutations of the ATP binding cassette subfamily C member (ABCC2) gene. The ABCC2 gene provides instructions for producing a protein called multidrug resistance protein 2 (MRP2) that works as a transporter protein. The MRP2 protein is involved in the transport of substances out of cells and is essential for the secretion of conjugated bilirubin out of the hepatocytes into the bile duct system for excretion. Mutation of the ABCC2 gene causes a deficiency of canalicular MRP2 expression and impairs the transport of conjugated bilirubin into the bile duct system. Conjugated bilirubin accumulates in hepatocytes, and conjugated bilirubin levels rise in the blood. [2] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Metabolism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Ultimately, LDL can be recycled in the liver cells’ Golgi apparatus to make more lipoproteins, or it can get excreted through bile. The “empty” HDL can enter the circulation to pick up excess cholesterol from the tissues. This transfer is mediated by ABCA1, and mutations to ABCA1 genes carry links with low serum HDL cholesterol and familial HDL deficiency, Tangiers Disease. HDL can also acquire cholesterol from cells via SR-B1 or passive diffusion and directly transport cholesterol to the liver by interacting with hepatic SR-B1 or indirectly by transferring cholesterol to VLDL or LDL, facilitated by CETP. Lecithin cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT) which is activated by apoA1 on HDL and mediates the esterification of cholesterol in HDL. Other ABC transporters may carry implications in cholesterol metabolism. Sitosterolemia is a rare genetic disorder that presents with elevated plasma levels of plant sterols, including sitosterol and premature atherosclerosis. Mutations to the ABC half transporters, ABCG5 and ABCG8, can increase intestinal uptake of plant sterols since there is a failure to efflux plant sterols into the lumen. Overexpression of the ABCG5 and 8 lead to increased biliary cholesterol secretion and lead researchers to suspect that they may regulate biliary cholesterol secretion. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Brain Barrier -- Mechanism -- Efflux Pumps
retrieved_docs.content: Efflux pumps are responsible for expelling both endogenous and exogenous compounds, such as drugs, from the brain into the blood. Of particular importance, active drug efflux transporters of the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) gene family are important determinants of drug distribution to and elimination from the CNS. This mechanism is seen as a significant obstacle in that it can limit the distribution of medications that are beneficial to treat CNS diseases. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Bile -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The Golgi apparatus, microtubules, and microfilaments in the hepatocytes participate in bile formation. The Golgi complex provides the vesicles for the substances secreted into the bile. The ATP-binding-cassette (ABC) that is newly synthesized is transferred from the Golgi complex to the canalicular membrane to function as a transporter. This process needs intact microtubules and microfilaments. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are also considered members, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early glucose dysregulation. The disorder group is mapped to familial hyperlipidemia type IIa (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a well‑characterized lipid abnormality that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, as indicated by the relationship between Autosomal dominant inheritance and فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ. This interconnected framework underscores how lipid metabolism disorders, specific hyperlipoproteinemia subtypes, and early diabetic manifestations are linked both pathophysiologically and genetically, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 335
retrieved_docs.content: Substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action represents a broad class of pharmacologic agents that block the activity of peptide hormone receptors. Within this overarching category, Substance with receptor antagonist mechanism of action (substance) is identified as a more specific subclass of agents that exert their therapeutic effect by antagonizing receptors in general. A particular example of this subclass is antagonisté CGRP receptorů, which comprises compounds that specifically inhibit the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor. Thus, CGRP receptor antagonists are situated under the umbrella of receptor‑antagonist substances, which in turn are encompassed by the larger group of peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists. This hierarchical organization clarifies how these drug classes relate to one another and aids clinicians in understanding the scope of receptor‑targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 142
retrieved_docs.content: within diuretic classifications. Collectively, this network connects chemical substructures, formulation hierarchies, therapeutic classes, mechanistic actions, disease indications (notably cystic fibrosis), and key contraindications, providing an integrated view for informed clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1951
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS are classified as liver therapy agents with lipotropic properties, meaning they are used to support hepatic function and promote the metabolism and transport of fats within the liver.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené” (meaning congenital or hereditary) is recognized as an allowed descriptor for both Alzheimer’s‑type dementia (الخرف من نوع الزهايمر) and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Applying “vrozené” to Alzheimer’s‑type dementia highlights the existence of early‑onset or familial forms of the disease that arise from genetic factors, while its use with hyperlipoproteinemia denotes the inherited variants of lipid metabolism disorders. Recognizing these hereditary forms aids clinicians in genetic counseling, risk assessment, and tailoring management strategies for patients who present with a congenital basis for either neurodegenerative or metabolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are recognized as a specific form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified under hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are considered member conditions, highlighting the close link between lipid abnormalities and early disturbances in carbohydrate metabolism. Laboratory results that are “above reference range” are interpreted as indicative of a disorder of lipid metabolism, underscoring the diagnostic relevance of lipid panels. The disorder “فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ” (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to the broader concept of disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, indicating that a single copy of the pathogenic allele can transmit the condition. Moreover, carbohydrate metabolism disorders (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) are positioned as a parent category to metabolic disorders (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي), reflecting the hierarchical organization of metabolic disease entities. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders intersect with broader metabolic pathways, genetic inheritance, and clinical interpretation, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 529
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that encompass several specific entities. Within this category, اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) are recognized as subtypes, indicating that these conditions are forms of lipid metabolism disorders. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset diabetes) is listed as a member of disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early diabetes. The condition فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, emphasizing the genetic basis of this specific lipid disorder. Furthermore, اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات (carbohydrate metabolism disorder) is identified as having اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي as its parent, linking carbohydrate metabolic abnormalities to the broader metabolic disorder framework. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid and carbohydrate metabolic disorders are interrelated, how specific lipid abnormalities can be genetically inherited, and how they may present clinically alongside early diabetic changes, informing a comprehensive approach to patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2780
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are a specific category of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified as hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). These conditions involve abnormalities in the synthesis, transport, or breakdown of lipids, leading to elevated blood lipid levels and associated risks such as atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease. Recognizing disorders of lipid metabolism as both a metabolic disturbance and a form of hyperlipoproteinemia underscores their role in systemic metabolic health and guides targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

Overall, the knowledge base delineates a hierarchical organization of bile‑therapy drugs, highlights several compounds with undefined molecular mechanisms, clarifies the classification of fat‑soluble vitamins (distinguishing vitamin K from retinoids), and groups specific musculo‑skeletal agents within their ATC category, all of which are relevant for understanding drug classification, therapeutic indications, and areas where mechanistic research remains needed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1608
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are classified as a form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). This category is mapped to type IIa hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a condition that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (Autosomal dominant inheritance). Recognizing that type IIa hyperlipidemia falls under the broader umbrella of lipid metabolism disorders and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians with diagnosis, genetic counseling, and the management of patients who present with elevated blood lipids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

Overall, the ATC hierarchy illustrates how ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS intersect with anabolic, bile‑related, and enzyme‑replacement therapies, and how these interrelated categories facilitate organized prescribing, research, and patient‑care strategies across a spectrum of digestive and metabolic conditions.
